questions.question: A 0.10 mol/L solution of a strong acid has a pH of:

choices.explanation: For a strong acid that completely dissociates: [H⁺] = 0.10 mol/L = 1.0 × 10⁻¹ mol/L; pH = −log(1.0 × 10⁻¹) = 1.
pH = 2 corresponds to [H⁺] = 0.010 mol/L; a 0.10 mol/L strong acid gives [H⁺] = 0.10 mol/L and pH = 1.
pH = 7 is neutral; a strong acid solution is acidic with pH < 7.
pH = 13 corresponds to pOH = 1, [OH⁻] = 0.10 mol/L; this is a 0.10 mol/L strong base, not acid.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following correctly defines a Brønsted-Lowry base?

choices.text: A species that produces $\text{OH}^-$ ions in water
choices.explanation: This is the Arrhenius definition of a base, which is more limited; Brønsted-Lowry theory extends beyond species that produce OH⁻ in water.

choices.text: A species that accepts a proton ($\text{H}^+$) from an acid
choices.explanation: The Brønsted-Lowry definition of a base is a proton acceptor; it receives H⁺ from the acid in a reaction.

choices.text: A species that donates an electron pair to an acid
choices.explanation: This is the Lewis base definition, which is even broader than Brønsted-Lowry; Brønsted-Lowry specifically refers to proton transfer.

choices.text: A species with more OH⁻ ions than H⁺ ions at equilibrium
choices.explanation: This describes a basic solution, not the definition of a Brønsted-Lowry base (which can be any proton acceptor, even in non-aqueous systems).

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: The $K_a$ of acetic acid ($\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$) is $1.8 \times 10^{-5}$. Which of the following correctly calculates the pH of a 0.10 mol/L acetic acid solution?

choices.explanation: pH = 1.0 would require [H⁺] = 0.10 mol/L, which would be the case for a strong acid; acetic acid is weak and only partially dissociates, giving a higher pH.
4.74 = pKa of acetic acid; pH = pKa only at the half-equivalence point in a titration when [HA] = [A⁻], not for a 0.10 mol/L weak acid solution.
pH ≈ 5.0 would require [H⁺] = 10⁻⁵ mol/L; for 0.10 mol/L acetic acid, the approximation gives [H⁺] ≈ 1.34 × 10⁻³ mol/L → pH ≈ 2.87.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: At 25°C, $K_w = 1.0 \times 10^{-14}$. In a basic solution where $[\text{OH}^-] = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ mol/L, what is the pH?

choices.explanation: pH = 3 would mean [H⁺] = 10⁻³ mol/L, which is acidic; the given solution is basic (pOH = 3, so pH = 11).
pH = 7 indicates a neutral solution; [OH⁻] = 10⁻³ mol/L is significantly greater than 10⁻⁷ mol/L, making the solution basic.
pH = 14 would correspond to [H⁺] = 10⁻¹⁴ mol/L and [OH⁻] = 1.0 mol/L; the given [OH⁻] = 10⁻³ mol/L gives pH = 11.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Based on the table, which of the following correctly ranks these acids from strongest to weakest?

choices.text: HCN > $\text{H}_2\text{CO}_3$ > $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$ > HF
choices.explanation: Acid strength increases with $K_a$; HCN has the smallest $K_a$ and is the weakest acid, not the strongest.

choices.text: HF > $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$ > $\text{H}_2\text{CO}_3$ > HCN
choices.explanation: Larger $K_a$ indicates stronger acid. In order: $K_a$(HF) = 7.2×10⁻⁴ > $K_a$(CH₃COOH) = 1.8×10⁻⁵ > $K_a$(H₂CO₃) = 4.3×10⁻⁷ > $K_a$(HCN) = 6.2×10⁻¹⁰.

choices.text: $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$ > HF > HCN > $\text{H}_2\text{CO}_3$
choices.explanation: HF has $K_a$ = 7.2×10⁻⁴, which is larger than $K_a$(CH₃COOH) = 1.8×10⁻⁵; HF is stronger than acetic acid.

choices.text: HF > HCN > $\text{H}_2\text{CO}_3$ > $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$
choices.explanation: This ordering incorrectly places HCN ($K_a$ = 6.2×10⁻¹⁰) above H₂CO₃ ($K_a$ = 4.3×10⁻⁷); H₂CO₃ is stronger than HCN.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: A buffer is most effective at resisting pH changes when:

choices.text: The pH is far from the $pK_a$ of the weak acid
choices.explanation: Buffers are most effective near pH = pKa; at pH values far from pKa, one component of the buffer pair is nearly exhausted.

choices.text: The ratio of $[\text{A}^-]/[\text{HA}]$ is close to 1 (i.e., pH ≈ $pK_a$)
choices.explanation: The Henderson-Hasselbalch equation shows that when [A⁻]/[HA] ≈ 1, both the acid and conjugate base are present in roughly equal amounts, providing maximum buffering capacity.

choices.text: The buffer contains a strong acid and its conjugate base
choices.explanation: Strong acids fully dissociate and have no appreciable conjugate base equilibrium; buffers require a weak acid (or weak base) component.

choices.text: The total buffer concentration is very low, spreading the acid and base components thinly
choices.explanation: Higher buffer concentration provides greater capacity to absorb acid or base; very dilute buffers have low capacity and are less effective.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content:
The Henderson-Hasselbalch equation is: $\text{pH} = pK_a + \log\dfrac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$
A buffer is prepared using acetic acid ($pK_a = 4.74$) with $[\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}] = 0.20$ mol/L and $[\text{CH}_3\text{COO}^-] = 0.40$ mol/L.

questions.question: What is the pH of this buffer?

choices.explanation: 4.44 = 4.74 + log(0.5) = 4.74 − 0.30 = 4.44; this would apply if [A⁻]/[HA] = 0.5 (i.e., [A⁻] < [HA]). The given ratio is 0.40/0.20 = 2.0 (A⁻ > HA), giving pH > pKa.
4.74 = pKa, which applies when [A⁻] = [HA]; the given solution has [A⁻] = 2[HA], so pH > pKa.
3.44 is far too low for an acetic acid buffer; it would require [H⁺] ≈ 0.0036 mol/L, which is not consistent with a buffered solution near pH 5.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: In the autoionization of water, $\text{H}_2\text{O}(l) \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+(aq) + \text{OH}^-(aq)$, $K_w = [\text{H}^+][\text{OH}^-] = 1.0 \times 10^{-14}$ at 25°C. Which of the following correctly describes a neutral solution?

choices.explanation: This describes an acidic solution, not a neutral one.

choices.text: $[\text{H}^+] = [\text{OH}^-] = 1.0 \times 10^{-7}$ mol/L; pH = 7
choices.explanation: In a neutral solution, [H⁺] = [OH⁻]. With $K_w$ = [H⁺][OH⁻] = 10⁻¹⁴: [H⁺] = [OH⁻] = √(10⁻¹⁴) = 10⁻⁷ mol/L; pH = 7.
This describes a basic solution where [OH⁻] > [H⁺]; a neutral solution requires equal concentrations.

choices.text: $[\text{H}^+] = 0$; the solution contains no protons
choices.explanation: Water always autoionizes to produce a small but non-zero [H⁺] = 10⁻⁷ mol/L; complete absence of H⁺ is not physically possible in an aqueous solution.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

title.text: Titration Curve: 0.10 mol/L CH₃COOH with 0.10 mol/L NaOH

questions.question: Based on the titration curve, which of the following correctly identifies the equivalence point and the half-equivalence point?

choices.text: Equivalence point: 25 mL (pH = 4.74); half-equivalence point: 50 mL (pH = 7.00)
choices.explanation: The equivalence point is where all acid is neutralized; pH = 4.74 at 25 mL is the half-equivalence point (pH = pKa). The equivalence point occurs at 50 mL (pH ≈ 8.7, not 7).

choices.text: Equivalence point: 50 mL (pH > 7); half-equivalence point: 25 mL (pH = 4.74 = $pK_a$)
choices.explanation: The equivalence point is where equal moles of acid and base react (50 mL); pH > 7 because the salt (sodium acetate) is basic due to hydrolysis. At 25 mL (half-equivalence), [HA] = [A⁻] and pH = pKa = 4.74.

choices.text: Equivalence point: 40 mL (pH = 7.00); half-equivalence point: 20 mL (pH = 4.74)
choices.explanation: pH = 7 at the equivalence point occurs only for strong acid/strong base titrations; for a weak acid, the equivalence point pH is above 7.

choices.text: Equivalence point: 0 mL (pH = 2.87); half-equivalence point: 25 mL (pH = 4.74)
choices.explanation: 0 mL is the starting point (pure acid, not the equivalence point); the equivalence point is at 50 mL.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: The $K_a$ of acetic acid is $1.8 \times 10^{-5}$. What is the $K_b$ for the acetate ion ($\text{CH}_3\text{COO}^-$)?

choices.explanation: Ka and Kb of a conjugate pair are not equal unless Kw = 1; they are related by Ka × Kb = Kw.
1.0 × 10⁻¹⁴ is Kw; Kb is calculated as Kw/Ka = 5.6 × 10⁻¹⁰.
1.8 × 10⁹ would result from Kb = Ka/Kw (inverting the correct relationship); the correct formula is Kb = Kw/Ka.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following is the strongest acid?

choices.explanation: H₃PO₄ is a weak acid with Ka = 7.5 × 10⁻³, much smaller than HClO₄ which is a strong acid.
HF is a weak acid (Ka = 7.2 × 10⁻⁴); despite common misconceptions, HF is not a strong acid.

choices.text: $\text{HClO}_4$ (perchloric acid)
choices.explanation: HClO₄ is one of the strongest known acids — a strong acid that fully dissociates in water. Its Ka >> 1, far exceeding the values for the other choices.

choices.text: $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$ ($K_a = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$)
choices.explanation: Acetic acid is a weak acid with Ka = 1.8 × 10⁻⁵; it is a much weaker acid than HClO₄.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: A student prepares an acetic acid/acetate buffer. She adds 0.010 mol of NaOH (strong base) to 1.0 L of a buffer containing 0.10 mol of acetic acid and 0.10 mol of sodium acetate.

questions.question: What happens to the pH of the buffer after the addition, and why?

choices.text: pH increases dramatically because the strong base overwhelms the buffer
choices.explanation: The buffer neutralizes the added base; 0.010 mol NaOH converts 0.010 mol HA to A⁻, leaving [HA] = 0.090 and [A⁻] = 0.11 mol — well within the buffer range.

choices.text: pH increases slightly; the NaOH converts some HA to A⁻, shifting the ratio $[\text{A}^-]/[\text{HA}]$ upward
pH decreases slightly because the addition of NaOH increases the ionic strength
choices.explanation: Adding base increases pH, not decreases it; and ionic strength effects are secondary to the buffer equilibrium.

choices.text: pH remains exactly constant because the buffer has infinite capacity
choices.explanation: No buffer has infinite capacity; adding base does shift the [A⁻]/[HA] ratio, causing a small pH increase. The change is small but not zero.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: A solution with $[\text{OH}^-] = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$ mol/L at 25°C has a pH of:

choices.explanation: 4.60 is neither pOH nor pH for this solution; pOH = 3.60 and pH = 10.40.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: Phenolphthalein indicator changes color from colorless (acid form, HIn) to pink (base form, In⁻) in the pH range 8.2–10.0. Its $pK_a = 9.1$.

questions.question: At pH = 9.1, which of the following best describes the indicator?

choices.text: Colorless, because pH < 10.0
choices.explanation: At pH = pKa = 9.1, [HIn] = [In⁻]; the solution is mid-transition (pale pink), not colorless, because half the indicator is in the colored In⁻ form.

choices.text: The indicator is mid-transition with roughly equal amounts of HIn and In⁻, appearing faint pink
choices.explanation: At pH = pKa, the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation gives log([In⁻]/[HIn]) = 0, so [In⁻] = [HIn]; the solution appears pale pink (intermediate between colorless and full pink).

choices.text: Fully pink, because pH = 9.1 is within the color change range of 8.2–10.0
choices.explanation: Full pink color requires pH >> pKa (essentially all in the In⁻ form); at pH = pKa, only half is converted to In⁻.

choices.text: Colorless, because pKa > 7 indicates the indicator is a weak acid that stays in the HIn form
choices.explanation: The pKa value does not determine whether HIn predominates at pH = pKa; at pH = pKa specifically, [HIn] = [In⁻] (equal amounts of both forms).

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following aqueous salt solutions is basic at 25°C?

choices.text: $\text{NaCl}$
choices.explanation: NaCl is the salt of a strong acid (HCl) and strong base (NaOH); neither Na⁺ nor Cl⁻ hydrolyzes significantly, giving a neutral solution (pH = 7).

choices.text: $\text{NH}_4\text{Cl}$
choices.explanation: NH₄Cl is the salt of a weak base (NH₃) and strong acid (HCl); NH₄⁺ hydrolyzes to give H⁺, making the solution acidic (pH < 7).

choices.text: $\text{CH}_3\text{COONa}$
choices.explanation: Sodium acetate is the salt of a weak acid (CH₃COOH) and strong base (NaOH); the acetate ion hydrolyzes water to produce OH⁻, making the solution basic (pH > 7).

choices.text: $\text{KNO}_3$
choices.explanation: KNO₃ is the salt of a strong acid (HNO₃) and strong base (KOH); neither K⁺ nor NO₃⁻ hydrolyzes, giving a neutral solution.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Using the data in the table, what is the $K_b$ for the fluoride ion?

choices.explanation: 7.2 × 10⁻⁴ is Ka of HF, not Kb of F⁻; they are related by Ka × Kb = Kw.
7.2 × 10⁻¹⁸ would result from Ka × Kw rather than Kw/Ka; the correct formula is Kb = Kw/Ka.
1.0 × 10⁻¹⁴ is Kw, not Kb(F⁻); Kb is calculated as Kw/Ka(HF) = 1.4 × 10⁻¹¹.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following species is amphoteric (can act as both an acid and a base)?

choices.text: $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$
choices.explanation: H₂SO₄ is a diprotic strong acid; it can only donate protons (act as an acid), not accept them.

choices.text: $\text{Na}^+$
choices.explanation: Na⁺ is a spectator ion from a strong base; it neither donates nor accepts protons in water and is neither acidic nor basic.

choices.text: $\text{HCO}_3^-$
choices.explanation: HCO₃⁻ can donate a proton (acting as acid: HCO₃⁻ → H⁺ + CO₃²⁻) or accept a proton (acting as base: HCO₃⁻ + H⁺ → H₂CO₃). It is amphoteric.

choices.text: $\text{Cl}^-$
choices.explanation: Cl⁻ is the conjugate base of a strong acid (HCl); it has essentially no tendency to accept a proton and acts only as a spectator ion in water.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: A student performs an experiment in which 25.0 mL of 0.100 mol/L weak acid HA ($pK_a = 5.00$) is titrated with 0.100 mol/L NaOH. The student records pH at various volumes of NaOH.

questions.question: At what volume of NaOH added does the pH equal $pK_a$, and what is the name of this point?

choices.text: 12.5 mL; the equivalence point
choices.explanation: At 12.5 mL (half-equivalence point), [HA] = [A⁻] and pH = pKa = 5.00; this is the half-equivalence point, not the equivalence point.

choices.text: 12.5 mL; the half-equivalence point
choices.explanation: At 12.5 mL, half the acid (0.00125 mol) has been neutralized; [HA] = [A⁻]. By Henderson-Hasselbalch: pH = pKa + log(1) = pKa. This is the half-equivalence point.

choices.text: 25.0 mL; the equivalence point
choices.explanation: At 25.0 mL, all the acid is neutralized (equivalence point); pH > 7 (the conjugate base A⁻ hydrolyzes water). pH = pKa occurs at 12.5 mL (half-equivalence point).

choices.text: 0 mL; the initial point
choices.explanation: At 0 mL NaOH, only HA is present; pH < pKa for a weak acid with [HA] = 0.1 mol/L. pH = pKa only when half the acid has been neutralized.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: The percent dissociation of a weak acid decreases when the concentration of the acid is increased. Which of the following best explains this trend?

choices.text: Increasing concentration provides more molecules, but Ka is fixed; the fraction that can dissociate decreases (common-ion-like dilution effect)
choices.explanation: For HA ⇌ H⁺ + A⁻: percent dissociation = [H⁺]/[HA]₀ × 100%. As [HA]₀ increases, [H⁺] increases but less than proportionally (because Ka = [H⁺]²/[HA] is fixed). Thus x/C₀ decreases as C₀ increases.

choices.text: Higher acid concentration increases the rate of the reverse reaction, suppressing dissociation
choices.explanation: While higher product concentration does suppress dissociation, the fundamental reason is that Ka is fixed; as C₀ increases, the fraction dissociated decreases even though the absolute amount of H⁺ increases.

choices.text: Ka decreases at higher concentrations due to interionic interactions
choices.explanation: Ka is a thermodynamic constant at fixed temperature; it does not change with concentration (activity coefficients cause small deviations but the fundamental trend is not due to Ka changing).

choices.text: Percent dissociation and concentration are unrelated quantities
choices.explanation: They are related; for a weak acid HA with Ka = x²/(C₀ − x) ≈ x²/C₀, percent dissociation = (x/C₀) × 100% ≈ √(Ka/C₀) × 100%, which clearly decreases as C₀ increases.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following best explains why $\text{NH}_3$ is a weak base rather than a strong base?

choices.text: $\text{NH}_3$ is a gas and does not ionize in water
choices.explanation: NH₃ does ionize in water: NH₃ + H₂O ⇌ NH₄⁺ + OH⁻; it is a weak base because this equilibrium lies far to the left.

choices.text: $\text{NH}_3$ does not contain an OH group, so it cannot produce OH⁻ ions
choices.explanation: NH₃ does produce OH⁻ by accepting H⁺ from water; Brønsted-Lowry bases do not require an OH group.

choices.text: The equilibrium $\text{NH}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{NH}_4^+ + \text{OH}^-$ lies mostly to the left ($K_b = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$)
choices.explanation: Kb = 1.8 × 10⁻⁵ << 1; the equilibrium strongly favors NH₃ and H₂O over NH₄⁺ and OH⁻. Only a small fraction of NH₃ molecules accept protons at any given time.

choices.text: $\text{NH}_3$ reacts with acids to form salts, making it acidic overall
choices.explanation: Reacting with acids is exactly what a base does; NH₃ reacting with HCl to form NH₄Cl is a base-acid neutralization reaction, confirming NH₃ is a base.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Based on the table, why is the pH at the equivalence point greater than 7 when a weak acid is titrated with a strong base?

choices.text: The strong base is always in slight excess at the equivalence point
choices.explanation: At the equivalence point, exactly stoichiometric amounts of acid and base have reacted; neither is in excess.

choices.text: The conjugate base of the weak acid (A⁻) hydrolyzes water to produce OH⁻, raising the pH above 7
choices.explanation: At the equivalence point, the solution contains A⁻ (the conjugate base). A⁻ + H₂O ⇌ HA + OH⁻; this hydrolysis produces OH⁻, giving pH > 7.

choices.text: The weak acid is not fully neutralized at the equivalence point, leaving excess acid that reacts with the base
choices.explanation: By definition, at the equivalence point all the acid has been neutralized; the question is about the pH of the resulting salt solution, not about incomplete neutralization.

choices.text: The pH is greater than 7 because the resulting solution contains Na⁺, which hydrolyzes water to produce $\text{H}^+$
choices.explanation: Na⁺ is the cation of a strong base (NaOH) and does not hydrolyze water appreciably; it is a spectator ion that has essentially no effect on pH. Only the conjugate base A⁻ affects the pH at the equivalence point.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: When designing a buffer system to maintain pH ≈ 7.4 (blood pH), which of the following weak acids would be the most appropriate choice?

choices.text: Oxalic acid ($pK_{a1} = 1.23$)
choices.explanation: A buffer is most effective when pH ≈ pKa; pKa = 1.23 is far from 7.4, making oxalic acid an inappropriate choice.

choices.text: Acetic acid ($pK_a = 4.74$)
choices.explanation: pKa = 4.74 is about 2.7 units from the target pH of 7.4; buffers work best within ±1 pH unit of pKa.

choices.text: Carbonic acid ($pK_{a2} = 6.35$ for $\text{HCO}_3^-$/$\text{CO}_3^{2-}$)
choices.explanation: pKa2 = 6.35 is about 1 pH unit below 7.4; closer but still not optimal. The HCO₃⁻/H₂CO₃ pair (pKa1 ≈ 6.1) is what blood uses, but this choice refers to pKa2.

choices.text: Dihydrogen phosphate ($\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4^-$/$\text{HPO}_4^{2-}$, $pK_{a2} = 7.20$)
choices.explanation: pKa2 of phosphoric acid = 7.20, which is within 0.2 pH units of the target pH of 7.4; this gives a buffer with excellent capacity at physiological pH. Phosphate buffers are widely used in biochemistry.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following correctly explains why HI is a stronger acid than HF in aqueous solution?

choices.text: HI has a larger molecular size, so it is more polar than HF
choices.explanation: HF is actually more polar than HI; the greater electronegativity of F creates a larger dipole. Polarity favors HF, not HI, in terms of making the bond polar.

choices.text: The H–I bond is weaker and longer than the H–F bond, making it easier to break and release H⁺
choices.explanation: Despite F being more electronegative (which would favor ionization of HF), the H–I bond is much weaker (lower bond energy) than H–F. Bond strength is the dominant factor; the weaker H–I bond breaks more easily, releasing H⁺.

choices.text: Iodide is a better leaving group because it is more electronegative than fluoride
choices.explanation: Fluorine is far more electronegative than iodine; however, iodide is a better leaving group because of its large size and high polarizability, not electronegativity.

choices.text: HF forms a special stable compound with water that reduces its ability to donate protons
choices.explanation: While HF does hydrogen bond strongly with water and its fluoride ion forms FHF⁻ dimers, the primary reason HI is a stronger acid is the much weaker H–I bond energy.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content:
A student measures the pH at three points during a titration of 25.0 mL of 0.100 mol/L weak base ($\text{NH}_3$, $K_b = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$) with 0.100 mol/L HCl:
- Before adding HCl: pH = 11.13
- After adding 12.5 mL HCl: pH = 9.26
- After adding 25.0 mL HCl: pH = 5.28

questions.question: Which of the following correctly explains the pH = 5.28 at the equivalence point?

choices.text: The equivalence point has pH < 7 because HCl is a strong acid, making the solution acidic
choices.explanation: At the equivalence point, no excess HCl remains; the acidity comes from hydrolysis of NH₄⁺, not from free HCl.

choices.text: At the equivalence point, all NH₃ has been converted to $\text{NH}_4^+$ (a weak acid); $\text{NH}_4^+$ donates a proton to water, producing an acidic solution
choices.explanation: NH₄⁺ + H₂O ⇌ NH₃ + H₃O⁺; Ka(NH₄⁺) = Kw/Kb = 5.6×10⁻¹⁰. The resulting acidic solution has pH = 5.28.

choices.text: The solution is acidic because water donates protons to Cl⁻ at the equivalence point
choices.explanation: Cl⁻ (conjugate base of strong acid HCl) has essentially no tendency to accept protons from water; the pH is determined by NH₄⁺ hydrolysis.

choices.text: pH = 5.28 indicates excess HCl was added beyond the equivalence point
choices.explanation: The problem states this pH is measured at the equivalence point (25.0 mL); the acidic pH results from NH₄⁺ hydrolysis, not excess acid.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: A student adds 100 mL of 0.10 mol/L NaOH to 100 mL of 0.10 mol/L HCl. Which of the following best predicts the pH of the resulting solution?

choices.text: pH = 1, because the strong acid dominates
choices.explanation: Equal moles of HCl (0.010 mol) and NaOH (0.010 mol) are mixed; they completely neutralize each other, leaving only NaCl and water.

choices.text: pH = 7, because equal moles of strong acid and strong base neutralize completely
choices.explanation: Moles HCl = (0.100 L)(0.10 mol/L) = 0.010 mol; moles NaOH = 0.010 mol. 1:1 neutralization gives NaCl (neutral salt) + H₂O → pH = 7.

choices.text: pH = 14, because NaOH is a strong base
choices.explanation: The NaOH is completely consumed by the HCl; no excess OH⁻ remains in solution.

choices.text: pH ≈ 6, because mixing two solutions dilutes both and slightly reduces pH
choices.explanation: Dilution alone would not make the solution acidic; the neutralization goes to completion regardless of dilution, giving pH = 7 for a strong acid/strong base mixture at stoichiometric equivalence.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: A student tests four unknown solutions with pH paper. Solution W has pH 2, Solution X has pH 6, Solution Y has pH 8, and Solution Z has pH 12.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly identifies the acidic and basic solutions?

choices.text: Only Solution W is acidic; only Solution Z is basic
choices.explanation: Solutions with pH < 7 are acidic; both W (pH 2) and X (pH 6) are acidic. Only Solutions Y and Z are basic.

choices.text: Solutions W and X are acidic; Solutions Y and Z are basic
choices.explanation: pH < 7 is acidic: W (pH 2) and X (pH 6). pH > 7 is basic: Y (pH 8) and Z (pH 12). pH = 7 is neutral.

choices.text: All four solutions are acidic because they all have pH less than 14
choices.explanation: The acidic/basic classification is based on whether pH is below or above 7, not whether pH is below 14. Solutions Y and Z (pH > 7) are basic.

choices.text: Solutions W and Z are acidic; Solutions X and Y are neutral
choices.explanation: Solution Z (pH 12) is strongly basic, not acidic. Solution X (pH 6) is slightly acidic, not neutral.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: A laboratory technician tests six common household chemicals and records their approximate pH values: vinegar (pH 2.4), baking soda solution (pH 8.3), bleach (pH 12), lemon juice (pH 2.0), milk of magnesia (pH 10.5), and pure water (pH 7.0).

questions.question: Which of the following is a strong acid?

choices.text: Acetic acid ($\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$)
choices.explanation: Acetic acid is a weak acid with $K_a = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$; it only partially dissociates in water.

choices.text: Hydrofluoric acid (HF)
choices.explanation: HF is a weak acid ($K_a \approx 7.2 \times 10^{-4}$) because the H-F bond is very strong and only partially breaks in water.

choices.text: Nitric acid ($\text{HNO}_3$)
choices.explanation: $\text{HNO}_3$ is one of the six strong acids (HCl, HBr, HI, $\text{HNO}_3$, $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$, $\text{HClO}_4$) that dissociate completely in water.

choices.text: Carbonic acid ($\text{H}_2\text{CO}_3$)
choices.explanation: Carbonic acid is a weak diprotic acid; it only partially dissociates ($K_{a1} \approx 4.3 \times 10^{-7}$).

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: A student looks up the Ka values of several weak acids: acetic acid (Ka = 1.8 x 10^-5), hypochlorous acid (Ka = 3.5 x 10^-8), and hydrocyanic acid (Ka = 6.2 x 10^-10). The student wants to find the Kb of each conjugate base using Kw = 1.0 x 10^-14.

questions.question: A weak acid HA has $K_a = 1.0 \times 10^{-5}$. Its conjugate base $\text{A}^-$ has a $K_b$ equal to:

choices.explanation: $K_a \times K_b = K_w = 1.0 \times 10^{-14}$. If $K_b$ equaled $K_a$, then $K_a \times K_b = 10^{-10}$, which is not $K_w$.
$K_b = K_w / K_a = (1.0 \times 10^{-14}) / (1.0 \times 10^{-5}) = 1.0 \times 10^{-9}$. A weak acid has a conjugate base that is a weak base.
$1.0 \times 10^{-14}$ is $K_w$ itself; $K_b = K_w / K_a$, which requires dividing $K_w$ by $K_a$.
This would result from multiplying $K_a \times K_w$, not from $K_b = K_w / K_a$.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: A student measures the pH of a 0.10 mol/L solution of hydrochloric acid (HCl) and a 0.10 mol/L solution of acetic acid (Ka = 1.8 x 10^-5) at the same temperature.

questions.question: Which of the following best explains why the acetic acid solution has a higher pH than the HCl solution at the same concentration?

choices.text: HCl has a higher molar mass than acetic acid
choices.explanation: Molar mass does not determine acid strength or pH. The key factor is the degree of dissociation in water.

choices.text: Acetic acid only partially dissociates in water, producing fewer $\text{H}^+$ ions than HCl at the same concentration
choices.explanation: HCl (strong acid) dissociates 100%, giving $[\text{H}^+] = 0.10$ mol/L, pH = 1. Acetic acid (weak acid) only partially dissociates, giving $[\text{H}^+] \approx 1.34 \times 10^{-3}$ mol/L, $\text{pH} \approx 2.87$.

choices.text: Acetic acid reacts with water to produce $\text{OH}^-$ ions, making it more basic
choices.explanation: Acetic acid reacts with water to donate $\text{H}^+$ ions, making the solution acidic. It produces $\text{H}_3\text{O}^+$, not $\text{OH}^-$.

choices.text: HCl dissociates into two ions while acetic acid dissociates into three ions
choices.explanation: Ion count per formula unit is not the determining factor. The critical difference is that HCl fully dissociates while acetic acid only partially dissociates.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: A chemistry student uses the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation to analyze buffer solutions in a blood pH regulation lab. The equation is: pH = pKa + log([A-]/[HA]). Blood plasma is buffered primarily by the carbonic acid/bicarbonate system: H2CO3 / HCO3- with pKa = 6.10.

questions.question: The Henderson-Hasselbalch equation is $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log\dfrac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$. At the half-equivalence point of a weak acid titration, the pH equals:

choices.explanation: pH = 7 at the equivalence point applies only to strong acid-strong base titrations, not weak acid titrations.

choices.text: $\text{p}K_a + \log(2)$
choices.explanation: At the half-equivalence point, $[\text{A}^-] = [\text{HA}]$, so the log term equals $\log(1) = 0$, not $\log(2)$.

choices.text: $\text{p}K_a$
choices.explanation: At the half-equivalence point, half the acid has been neutralized, so $[\text{A}^-] = [\text{HA}]$. Substituting: $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log(1) = \text{p}K_a + 0 = \text{p}K_a$.

choices.text: $\text{p}K_b$
choices.explanation: $\text{p}K_b$ is the negative log of the base dissociation constant. pH at the half-equivalence point equals $\text{p}K_a$ of the weak acid being titrated.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: A buffer solution is prepared by mixing 0.20 mol of formic acid (HCOOH, pKa = 3.74) and 0.20 mol of sodium formate (HCOONa) in 1.0 L of water.

questions.question: What is the pH of this buffer solution?

choices.explanation: Using Henderson-Hasselbalch: $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log([\text{A}^-]/[\text{HA}]) = 3.74 + \log(0.20/0.20) = 3.74 + \log(1) = 3.74$.
pH = 7 would require equal concentrations of conjugate pairs with $\text{p}K_a = 7$. Formic acid has $\text{p}K_a = 3.74$.
This is the pH of a 0.10 mol/L weak acid solution without buffer. Equal molar amounts of acid and conjugate base give pH = $\text{p}K_a$.
4.08 would result from an incorrect ratio. With equal moles of acid and conjugate base, the ratio is 1, $\log(1) = 0$, giving pH = $\text{p}K_a$.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: A buffer contains 0.10 mol/L of a weak acid (HA) and 0.10 mol/L of its conjugate base (A-). A small amount of strong acid (HCl) is added to the buffer.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes what happens when the strong acid is added?

choices.text: The pH drops dramatically because HCl is a strong acid
choices.explanation: A buffer resists pH changes. The conjugate base $\text{A}^-$ reacts with the added $\text{H}^+$: $\text{A}^- + \text{H}^+ \rightarrow \text{HA}$, absorbing the added acid and limiting pH change.

choices.text: The conjugate base reacts with the added $\text{H}^+$, consuming it and limiting the pH decrease
choices.explanation: Buffer action: $\text{A}^- + \text{H}^+ \rightarrow \text{HA}$. The conjugate base acts as a reservoir that neutralizes added acid, keeping pH nearly constant.

choices.text: The weak acid reacts with HCl, forming a neutral product
choices.explanation: Acids do not react with each other. It is the conjugate base ($\text{A}^-$) that reacts with the added $\text{H}^+$ to resist pH change.

choices.text: The buffer neutralizes the HCl completely so pH does not change at all
choices.explanation: Buffers resist but do not completely prevent pH changes. Adding acid shifts the ratio [A-]/[HA] slightly, causing a small decrease in pH.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: A biochemist needs to prepare four different solutions for an experiment: Solution 1 is made from HCl and NaCl. Solution 2 contains NH3 and NH4Cl. Solution 3 contains NaOH and NaCl. Solution 4 is made by mixing equal moles of HCl and NaOH.

questions.question: Which of the following pairs would make an effective buffer solution?

choices.text: HCl and NaCl
choices.explanation: HCl is a strong acid that dissociates completely; NaCl is a neutral salt. There is no weak acid/conjugate base pair to resist pH changes.

choices.text: $\text{NH}_3$ and $\text{NH}_4\text{Cl}$
choices.explanation: $\text{NH}_3$ (weak base) and $\text{NH}_4^+$ (its conjugate acid from $\text{NH}_4\text{Cl}$) form a buffer. NH3 neutralizes added acid; NH4+ neutralizes added base.

choices.text: NaOH and NaCl
choices.explanation: NaOH is a strong base that dissociates completely. Without a conjugate acid, there is no weak acid/base pair to buffer pH changes.

choices.text: HCl and NaOH in equal moles
choices.explanation: Mixing equal moles of strong acid and strong base produces NaCl and water - a neutral salt solution with no buffering capacity.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: A titration curve is plotted for the titration of 25.0 mL of 0.10 mol/L acetic acid (Ka = 1.8 x 10^-5) with 0.10 mol/L NaOH.

questions.question: At the equivalence point of this titration, which of the following best predicts the pH?

choices.text: pH = 7, because acid and base have fully neutralized each other
choices.explanation: pH = 7 at the equivalence point applies only to strong acid-strong base titrations. Here, the equivalence point contains acetate ion ($\text{CH}_3\text{COO}^-$), a weak base that hydrolyzes to give basic pH.

choices.text: pH < 7, because excess acetic acid remains
choices.explanation: At the equivalence point, no excess acetic acid remains - all of it has been neutralized by NaOH. The solution contains only sodium acetate.

choices.text: pH > 7, because the product (acetate ion) is a weak base that hydrolyzes water
choices.explanation: At the equivalence point, all acetic acid has been converted to sodium acetate. The acetate ion ($\text{CH}_3\text{COO}^-$) hydrolyzes: $\text{CH}_3\text{COO}^- + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CH}_3\text{COOH} + \text{OH}^-$, making the solution basic.

choices.text: pH = $\text{p}K_a = 4.74$, because this is the defining point of the titration
choices.explanation: pH = $\text{p}K_a$ occurs at the half-equivalence point, not at the equivalence point.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Sodium acetate ($\text{CH}_3\text{COONa}$) dissolves in water to produce a basic solution. Which of the following best explains this observation?

choices.text: The sodium ion reacts with water to produce $\text{OH}^-$ ions
choices.explanation: Na+ is the cation of a strong base (NaOH) and is a spectator ion - it does not react with water or affect pH.

choices.text: The acetate ion is the conjugate base of a weak acid and accepts a proton from water, producing $\text{OH}^-$
choices.explanation: Acetate (weak conjugate base) hydrolyzes: $\text{CH}_3\text{COO}^- + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CH}_3\text{COOH} + \text{OH}^-$. This increases $[\text{OH}^-]$, making the solution basic.

choices.text: The acetate ion donates a proton to water, producing $\text{H}_3\text{O}^+$
choices.explanation: Acetate is a base, not an acid; it accepts protons. Donating a proton would make the solution acidic, not basic.

choices.text: Sodium acetate is a strong electrolyte that increases ion concentration and therefore pH
choices.explanation: Being a strong electrolyte means complete dissociation into ions, but that alone does not determine pH. The basicity comes from acetate ion hydrolysis.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: The following salts are each dissolved in water at the same concentration: NH4Cl, NaCl, NaCH3COO, AlCl3.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly predicts the pH of each salt solution?

choices.text: $\text{NH}_4\text{Cl}$ acidic; NaCl neutral; $\text{NaCH}_3\text{COO}$ basic; $\text{AlCl}_3$ acidic
choices.explanation: NH4+ (conjugate acid of weak base NH3) hydrolyzes to give acidic solution. NaCl (strong acid + strong base) is neutral. CH3COO- (conjugate base of weak acid) hydrolyzes to give basic solution. Al3+ (small, highly charged metal ion) hydrolyzes to give acidic solution.

choices.text: All salts produce neutral solutions because salts are ionic compounds
choices.explanation: Salts from weak acid/base components undergo hydrolysis. Only salts from strong acid + strong base (like NaCl) are neutral.

choices.text: $\text{NH}_4\text{Cl}$ basic; NaCl neutral; $\text{NaCH}_3\text{COO}$ acidic; $\text{AlCl}_3$ neutral
choices.explanation: NH4Cl is acidic (NH4+ donates a proton), not basic. NaCH3COO is basic (CH3COO- accepts a proton), not acidic.

choices.text: All salts produce acidic solutions because dissolution releases ions into water
choices.explanation: Only salts with acidic cations or basic anions produce non-neutral solutions. NaCl dissolves into Na+ and Cl-, both spectator ions, giving neutral pH.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: A student compares the ionization of strong acids versus weak acids in water. For strong acids like HCl, the reaction HCl -> H+ + Cl- proceeds essentially to completion. For weak acids like HF, the reaction HF <-> H+ + F- reaches an equilibrium. The student notes that Ka values for strong acids are typically reported as "very large" or undefined, while weak acids have Ka values ranging from about 10^-1 to 10^-11.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly compares the $K_a$ values of a strong acid and a weak acid?

choices.text: Strong acids have $K_a < 1$; weak acids have $K_a > 1$
choices.explanation: This is backwards. Strong acids have very large $K_a$ (essentially $\gg 1$) because they dissociate completely; weak acids have $K_a \ll 1$.

choices.text: Strong acids have $K_a \gg 1$; weak acids have $K_a \ll 1$
choices.explanation: A large $K_a$ means the equilibrium strongly favors products (complete dissociation) - characteristic of strong acids. A small $K_a$ means partial dissociation - characteristic of weak acids.

choices.text: Strong acids have $K_a = 1$; weak acids have $K_a < 1$
choices.explanation: Strong acids do not have $K_a = 1$; their $K_a$ is essentially undefined because they dissociate completely.

choices.text: $K_a$ values are the same for all acids at a given temperature
choices.explanation: $K_a$ is an acid-specific constant. Different acids have different $K_a$ values at the same temperature.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: A titration curve of a weak base with a strong acid is plotted. The curve shows a gradual change in pH, a buffering region, and a steep drop near the equivalence point.

questions.question: At the equivalence point of this weak base-strong acid titration, which of the following correctly predicts the pH?

choices.text: pH = 7, because the acid and base have fully reacted
choices.explanation: pH = 7 at the equivalence point occurs only in strong acid-strong base titrations. Here, the conjugate acid of the weak base (e.g., NH4+) remains in solution and makes it acidic.

choices.text: pH > 7, because excess strong acid remains
choices.explanation: At the equivalence point there is no excess strong acid. The slightly acidic pH comes from hydrolysis of the conjugate acid, not from unreacted HCl.

choices.text: pH < 7, because the conjugate acid of the weak base hydrolyzes to release $\text{H}^+$
choices.explanation: At the equivalence point, the weak base has been fully converted to its conjugate acid (e.g., $\text{NH}_3 \rightarrow \text{NH}_4^+$). $\text{NH}_4^+$ hydrolyzes: $\text{NH}_4^+ \rightleftharpoons \text{NH}_3 + \text{H}^+$, making the solution acidic.

choices.text: pH = $\text{p}K_b$ of the weak base
choices.explanation: pH at the equivalence point is not equal to $\text{p}K_b$. The pH is determined by the $K_a$ of the conjugate acid.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Phosphoric acid ($\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4$) is a triprotic acid with $K_{a1} \gg K_{a2} \gg K_{a3}$. Which of the following best explains this pattern?

choices.text: Each successive dissociation removes a proton from an increasingly negative ion, making removal progressively harder
choices.explanation: After losing each $\text{H}^+$, the ion becomes more negatively charged: $\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{PO}_4^- \rightarrow \text{HPO}_4^{2-} \rightarrow \text{PO}_4^{3-}$. Removing a proton from an increasingly negative species requires more energy, so each $K_a$ is smaller.

choices.text: Each successive $K_a$ is smaller because the acid becomes weaker as concentration decreases during titration
choices.explanation: $K_a$ values are intrinsic properties of the acid, not dependent on concentration. The decreasing $K_a$ reflects increasing charge on the anion, not changing concentration.

choices.text: The three protons in $\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4$ have equal dissociation constants because they are all H-O bonds
choices.explanation: Although all three are O-H bonds, the electrostatic environment around each proton changes with each ionization step, leading to very different $K_a$ values.

choices.text: The decreasing $K_a$ pattern is unique to phosphoric acid and does not apply to other polyprotic acids
choices.explanation: Decreasing successive $K_a$ values is a general feature of all polyprotic acids, including $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$, $\text{H}_2\text{CO}_3$, and others.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: A chemistry student prepares four aqueous solutions at 25 degrees C: (1) 0.10 mol/L HCl, (2) 0.10 mol/L HNO3, (3) 0.10 mol/L HF (Ka = 7.2 x 10^-4), (4) 0.10 mol/L CH3COOH (Ka = 1.8 x 10^-5).

questions.question: Which of the following correctly ranks the solutions from lowest to highest pH?

choices.text: HCl = $\text{HNO}_3$ < HF < $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$
choices.explanation: HCl and HNO3 (strong acids) fully dissociate giving pH = 1 each. HF is a stronger weak acid ($K_a = 7.2 \times 10^{-4}$) giving lower pH than acetic acid ($K_a = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$). Ranking lowest to highest pH: HCl = HNO3 < HF < CH3COOH.

choices.text: $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$ < HF < HCl = $\text{HNO}_3$
choices.explanation: This is reversed. Strong acids (HCl, HNO3) have the lowest pH (highest H+ concentration) due to complete dissociation, not the weakest acids.

choices.text: HF < $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$ < HCl < $\text{HNO}_3$
choices.explanation: HCl and HNO3 are both strong acids at equal concentration, so they have the same (lowest) pH. And they have lower pH than either weak acid.

choices.text: All four solutions have the same pH because they have the same concentration
choices.explanation: Same concentration does not mean same pH. Strong acids dissociate 100%, giving much more H+ than weak acids at the same molarity.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: A solution has $[\text{OH}^-] = 1.0 \times 10^{-4}$ mol/L at 25 degrees C. What is the pH of this solution?

choices.explanation: pH = 4 corresponds to pOH = 10, meaning $[\text{OH}^-] = 10^{-10}$ mol/L - much lower than $10^{-4}$ mol/L.
pOH = $-\log(1.0 \times 10^{-4}) = 4$. pH = 14 - pOH = 14 - 4 = 10. The solution is basic (pH > 7) because $[\text{OH}^-]$ exceeds $[\text{H}^+]$.
pH = 14 corresponds to $[\text{OH}^-] = 1.0$ mol/L, a 1.0 mol/L strong base solution - much more basic than $10^{-4}$ mol/L.
pH = 7 is neutral, meaning $[\text{OH}^-] = [\text{H}^+] = 10^{-7}$ mol/L. A solution with $[\text{OH}^-] = 10^{-4}$ mol/L is basic (pH = 10).

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: A student titrates 50.0 mL of 0.10 mol/L weak acid HA (Ka = 1.0 x 10^-5) with 0.10 mol/L NaOH. The titration is monitored with a pH meter.

questions.question: At what volume of added NaOH does the buffer region of this titration reach its maximum buffering capacity?

choices.text: 0 mL, before any NaOH is added
choices.explanation: Before NaOH is added, there is only weak acid - no conjugate base present. Buffer capacity requires both weak acid and conjugate base components.

choices.text: 25.0 mL (half-equivalence point)
choices.explanation: Maximum buffer capacity occurs when $[\text{HA}] = [\text{A}^-]$, at the half-equivalence point. Here, 25.0 mL of NaOH converts exactly half the acid to conjugate base.

choices.text: 50.0 mL (equivalence point)
choices.explanation: At the equivalence point, all the weak acid has been converted to conjugate base. There is no weak acid remaining, so there is no buffer capacity.

choices.text: 100.0 mL, after the equivalence point
choices.explanation: After the equivalence point, excess strong base (NaOH) dominates. Buffer capacity is destroyed because the weak acid component has been exhausted.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the relationship between $K_a$ and $K_b$ for a conjugate acid-base pair at 25 degrees C?

choices.text: $K_a + K_b = K_w$
choices.explanation: The relationship involves multiplication, not addition: $K_a \times K_b = K_w = 1.0 \times 10^{-14}$ at 25 degrees C.

choices.text: $K_a \times K_b = K_w = 1.0 \times 10^{-14}$
choices.explanation: For any conjugate acid-base pair, the product of $K_a$ and $K_b$ always equals $K_w$ at 25 degrees C. This allows calculation of $K_b$ from $K_a$: $K_b = K_w / K_a$.

choices.text: $K_a = K_b$ for any conjugate pair
choices.explanation: $K_a = K_b$ only when both equal $\sqrt{K_w} = 10^{-7}$. In general, $K_a \neq K_b$ for a conjugate pair; their product equals $K_w$.

choices.text: $K_a / K_b = K_w$
choices.explanation: Division of $K_a$ by $K_b$ does not equal $K_w$ in general. The correct relationship is $K_a \times K_b = K_w$.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: Sulfuric acid (H2SO4) is a diprotic acid. The first dissociation is complete (strong acid behavior): H2SO4 -> H+ + HSO4-. The second dissociation is partial: HSO4- <-> H+ + SO4(2-), Ka2 = 1.2 x 10^-2.

questions.question: For a dilute 0.010 mol/L $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ solution, which of the following is most accurate about the pH?

choices.text: pH = 2.0 exactly, from the first dissociation only
choices.explanation: The first dissociation gives $[\text{H}^+] = 0.010$ mol/L, pH = 2.0. However, $K_{a2} = 0.012$ is significant at this low concentration and the second dissociation provides additional $\text{H}^+$, lowering pH below 2.0.

choices.text: pH < 2.0, because the second dissociation provides additional $\text{H}^+$ ions
choices.explanation: At 0.010 mol/L, $K_{a2} = 0.012$ is comparable to the concentration, so the second dissociation is significant. Total $[\text{H}^+]$ exceeds 0.010 mol/L, giving pH < 2.0.

choices.text: pH > 2.0, because partial dissociation of $\text{HSO}_4^-$ reduces total $\text{H}^+$ concentration
choices.explanation: The second dissociation adds more $\text{H}^+$ ions - it does not reduce $[\text{H}^+]$. pH is lower, not higher, than 2.0.

choices.text: pH = 1.0, because $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ always produces twice as many protons as HCl at the same concentration
choices.explanation: pH = 1.0 would require $[\text{H}^+] = 0.10$ mol/L. The first dissociation gives only 0.010 mol/L $\text{H}^+$; even with the second step, $[\text{H}^+]$ does not reach 0.10 mol/L at this dilution.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following correctly identifies an amphoteric species?

choices.text: $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ only
choices.explanation: Water is amphoteric, but it is not the only amphoteric species. $\text{HCO}_3^-$, $\text{HSO}_4^-$, and $\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4^-$ are also amphoteric.

choices.text: $\text{Cl}^-$, because it can combine with or release $\text{H}^+$
choices.explanation: Cl- is the conjugate base of the strong acid HCl. It does not react with water and is not amphoteric - it is a spectator ion in aqueous solution.

choices.text: $\text{HCO}_3^-$, because it can donate a proton (to become $\text{CO}_3^{2-}$) or accept a proton (to become $\text{H}_2\text{CO}_3$)
choices.explanation: An amphoteric species can act as both an acid and a base. $\text{HCO}_3^-$ donates $\text{H}^+$ to become $\text{CO}_3^{2-}$ (acting as acid) or accepts $\text{H}^+$ to become $\text{H}_2\text{CO}_3$ (acting as base).

choices.text: $\text{NaOH}$, because it contains both Na+ and OH- ions
choices.explanation: NaOH is a strong base only - Na+ is a spectator and OH- only accepts protons. NaOH cannot donate protons, so it is not amphoteric.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: A chemistry student compares two buffer solutions: Buffer I contains 0.50 mol/L acetic acid and 0.50 mol/L sodium acetate. Buffer II contains 0.050 mol/L acetic acid and 0.050 mol/L sodium acetate.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly compares the pH and buffering capacity of the two solutions?

choices.text: Buffer I has a higher pH than Buffer II; Buffer I has greater buffering capacity
choices.explanation: Both buffers have the same [A-]/[HA] ratio = 1, giving identical pH = pKa = 4.74. Only buffering capacity differs.

choices.text: Both buffers have the same pH; Buffer I has greater buffering capacity because it contains more moles of buffer components
choices.explanation: pH depends only on the ratio [A-]/[HA] (both = 1 here), so pH = pKa = 4.74 for both. Buffering capacity depends on absolute amounts; Buffer I has 10x more buffer components, so it can resist more added acid or base.

choices.text: Buffer II has a higher pH because dilute solutions are more basic
choices.explanation: Dilution does not change pH for a buffer with equal concentrations of weak acid and conjugate base - the ratio [A-]/[HA] stays constant at 1. pH = pKa regardless of concentration.

choices.text: Both buffers have the same pH and the same buffering capacity because they have the same pKa
choices.explanation: Same pKa means same pH because the ratio is also identical. However, buffering capacity depends on the number of moles of each component. Buffer I has greater capacity.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Hydrochloric acid (HCl) has a much larger $K_a$ than hydrofluoric acid (HF). What structural difference best explains the greater acid strength of HCl?

choices.text: The H-Cl bond is weaker than the H-F bond, making it easier to break and release $\text{H}^+$
choices.explanation: Bond enthalpy of H-F (~567 kJ/mol) is much greater than H-Cl (~432 kJ/mol). The stronger H-F bond requires more energy to break, so HF donates H+ much less readily than HCl, making HCl the stronger acid.

choices.text: Cl has a higher electronegativity than F, increasing the polarity of the H-Cl bond
choices.explanation: F is more electronegative than Cl (F: 4.0, Cl: 3.0). The greater polarity of H-F would suggest it should be a stronger acid, but the H-F bond strength dominates for these binary acids.

choices.text: HCl has a higher molar mass, making it more soluble in water
choices.explanation: Molar mass and solubility do not determine acid strength. HCl's greater acid strength relative to HF is due to bond strength.

choices.text: HCl molecules form stronger hydrogen bonds with water, facilitating ionization
choices.explanation: HF actually forms stronger hydrogen bonds with water than HCl. Yet HCl is the stronger acid because the weaker H-Cl bond is easier to break.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: A buffer solution has pH = 5.00 and is prepared using acetic acid (Ka = 1.8 x 10^-5, pKa = 4.74) and sodium acetate.

questions.question: What is the ratio $[\text{CH}_3\text{COO}^-]/[\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}]$ in this buffer?

choices.explanation: Using Henderson-Hasselbalch: $5.00 = 4.74 + \log([\text{A}^-]/[\text{HA}])$; $\log([\text{A}^-]/[\text{HA}]) = 0.26$; $[\text{A}^-]/[\text{HA}] = 10^{0.26} \approx 1.82$. Since pH > pKa, conjugate base dominates.
A ratio of 0.55 means [HA] > [A-], which gives pH < pKa. Since pH (5.00) > pKa (4.74), the conjugate base must exceed the acid.
A ratio of 1.00 gives pH = pKa = 4.74. The buffer pH is 5.00, higher than pKa, so the conjugate base exceeds the acid and the ratio is greater than 1.
$10^{0.26} \approx 1.82$, not 3.46. A ratio of 3.46 would give $\log(3.46) \approx 0.54$, meaning pH = 4.74 + 0.54 = 5.28, not 5.00.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following correctly predicts whether a 0.10 mol/L solution of ammonium chloride ($\text{NH}_4\text{Cl}$) is acidic, basic, or neutral?

choices.text: Neutral, because NH4Cl contains both a cation and anion that cancel each other
choices.explanation: Charge neutrality does not imply pH neutrality. The NH4+ cation is a weak acid (conjugate acid of the weak base NH3) that hydrolyzes water to give H+, making the solution acidic.

choices.text: Basic, because Cl- is a base that reacts with water to produce OH-
choices.explanation: Cl- is the conjugate base of the strong acid HCl. It is too weak a base to accept protons from water and does not affect pH.

choices.text: Acidic, because $\text{NH}_4^+$ is the conjugate acid of a weak base and donates $\text{H}^+$ to water
choices.explanation: $\text{NH}_4^+ \rightleftharpoons \text{NH}_3 + \text{H}^+$ - NH4+ donates a proton to water because NH3 is a weak base. This hydrolysis increases $[\text{H}^+]$, giving an acidic solution.

choices.text: Basic, because NH4Cl contains nitrogen, which is always basic in solution
choices.explanation: Not all nitrogen-containing species are basic in solution. NH4+ is acidic; the acidity or basicity depends on whether the ion accepts or donates protons.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: A student adds 10.0 mL of 0.10 mol/L HCl to 100.0 mL of a buffer made of 0.10 mol/L acetic acid (Ka = 1.8 x 10^-5) and 0.10 mol/L sodium acetate.

questions.question: Which of the following best predicts the effect of adding the HCl on the buffer pH?

choices.text: pH decreases slightly as some acetate ion is converted to acetic acid by the added $\text{H}^+$
choices.explanation: Reaction: $\text{CH}_3\text{COO}^- + \text{H}^+ \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$. This converts some $\text{A}^-$ to HA, decreasing the [A-]/[HA] ratio and slightly lowering pH per Henderson-Hasselbalch.

choices.text: pH drops dramatically to below 2
choices.explanation: The buffer resists dramatic pH change. Only a small amount of HCl (0.001 mol) is added relative to 0.010 mol each of acetic acid and acetate; the buffer absorbs it without dramatic change.

choices.text: pH increases because acetic acid neutralizes the HCl
choices.explanation: Adding acid (HCl) always decreases pH, never increases it. The acetate ion reacts with the added H+, limiting the decrease but not reversing it.

choices.text: pH remains exactly the same because buffers are immune to acid addition
choices.explanation: Buffers resist but do not completely prevent pH changes. The addition of HCl shifts the acid-base ratio slightly, causing a small, measurable decrease in pH.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following correctly identifies the conjugate base of $\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4^-$ in the Brønsted-Lowry sense?

choices.text: $\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4$
choices.explanation: $\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4$ is formed by adding a proton to $\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4^-$, making it the conjugate acid. The conjugate base is obtained by removing a proton.

A conjugate base is formed by removing one $\text{H}^+$ from the acid. $\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4^-$ loses one proton to give $\text{HPO}_4^{2-}$. The two species differ by exactly one proton, which is the definition of a conjugate acid-base pair.
$\text{PO}_4^{3-}$ results from removing all three ionizable protons from phosphoric acid, not just one from $\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4^-$. Conjugate pairs differ by exactly one proton.

choices.text: $\text{OH}^-$
choices.explanation: $\text{OH}^-$ is a common base but it is not the conjugate base of $\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4^-$. The conjugate base is produced when $\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4^-$ donates a proton, yielding $\text{HPO}_4^{2-}$.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: A 0.10 mol/L solution of a weak acid has a pH of 3.0. Another 0.10 mol/L solution of a different weak acid has a pH of 4.0.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly compares the Ka values of the two acids?

choices.text: The acid with pH 3.0 has a larger $K_a$ because lower pH means more $\text{H}^+$ produced (greater dissociation)
choices.explanation: Lower pH at the same concentration means more $[\text{H}^+]$, which means more dissociation and larger Ka. The acid with pH 3.0 has $[\text{H}^+] = 10^{-3}$ mol/L vs. $10^{-4}$ mol/L for pH 4.0, so its Ka is larger.

choices.text: The acid with pH 4.0 has a larger $K_a$ because higher pH means a stronger acid
choices.explanation: Higher pH means lower $[\text{H}^+]$, indicating less dissociation and a weaker acid (smaller Ka). Lower pH corresponds to stronger (more dissociated) weak acid.

choices.text: Both acids have equal $K_a$ values because they have the same concentration
choices.explanation: Same concentration does not give the same Ka. Ka is an intrinsic acid property; different Ka values can exist at any shared concentration.

choices.text: $K_a$ cannot be compared from pH data alone
choices.explanation: Ka can be estimated from pH and concentration: $K_a \approx [\text{H}^+]^2/C$ for a weak acid. Lower pH at equal concentration directly indicates a larger Ka.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
